     </ItemGroup>

data.content:
This project has **two custom targets** that break incremental builds:

---

### 1. `GenerateTimestamp` (line 6)
- **No `Inputs`/`Outputs`** — MSBuild always re-runs it.
- **Volatile content** — `$([System.DateTime]::Now.ToString())` produces a different value every build, so `build-timestamp.txt` always changes, which forces `CoreCompile` to re-run downstream.

### 2. `EmbedGitHash` (line 13)
- **No `Inputs`/`Outputs`** — always re-runs.
- **Unconditional overwrite** — `Overwrite="true"` updates the file timestamp of `git-hash.g.cs` on every build, even when the git hash hasn't changed. This also invalidates `CoreCompile`.

---

### Impact
Even with zero source code changes, **every build triggers a full recompilation** because both targets write files that are inputs to `CoreCompile`, and those files are modified every time.

### Fixes
| Target | Fix |
|---|---|
| `GenerateTimestamp` | Add `Inputs`/`Outputs` and remove the volatile `DateTime.Now` value (or accept that this target always runs, but write to a file that is **not** an input to compilation). |
| `EmbedGitHash` | Add `Inputs="$(ProjectDir).git/HEAD;$(ProjectDir).git/refs/heads/**"` and `Outputs="$(IntermediateOutputPath)git-hash.g.cs"` so MSBuild skips it when the hash is unchanged. Also use `WriteLinesToFile` with a diff-check or `Hash` task to avoid overwriting with identical content. |
data.reasoningText:
Now I can analyze the incremental build issues. There are two custom targets in this project, both with problems:

1. **`GenerateTimestamp`** — Writes the current date/time to `build-timestamp.txt` on every build. This is a volatile property (`$([System.DateTime]::Now.ToString())`) that changes every time, so the file always changes. This target has no `Inputs`/`Outputs` attributes, so MSBuild will always run it. And even if it did have Inputs/Outputs, the content changes every time, causing downstream targets (CoreCompile) to detect the changed file and rebuild.


2. **`EmbedGitHash`** — Runs `git rev-parse HEAD` to write the git hash to `git-hash.g.cs`. This target also lacks `Inputs`/`Outputs`, so it always executes. Even when the hash hasn't changed, it overwrites the file with `Overwrite="true"`, updating the timestamp and forcing CoreCompile to re-run unnecessarily.

Both targets are non-incremental by design, and the timestamp volatility in the first one guarantees constant rebuilds.
